data.title: Powell Industries: Valuation At Multi-Year Lows (Upgrade To Buy)
data.description:
Investment thesis

Powell Industries (NASDAQ:POWL) is a key company in trends needed for reliable electrical power systems. Since my previous coverage, the stock has fallen 20% and the valuation has adjusted considerably. However, there have been no fundamental changes and the company is not as at risk from tariffs or the cancellation of data center projects, hence my upgrade to Buy.

Data center projects weaken

In February, I covered Powell's Q1 2025 earnings, detailing the business's fundamentals. Some topics, such as the opportunity within the data center sector, were mentioned. In this regard, there have been updates regarding one of the risks I mentioned: Decreased utilization of data centers.

The Chinese ChatGPT competition stated that it built its model more efficiently, requiring fewer Nvidia GPUs and therefore lower power energy needed (like the one Powell provides).

At the end of March, Microsoft announced that it had abandoned data center projects in the United States and Europe. Subsequently, in early April, the news was confirmed that work on a campus in Jakarta, another in Chicago, and the expansion of its current data center in Wisconsin had been canceled. The stated reason was that current capacity is sufficient to meet anticipated demand, which could be influenced by the Deep Seek announcement I mentioned in my previous coverage.

Since March 25 (the day before the news broke), Powell's shares have fallen 12%. While this hasn't been the only reason, as we'll see later, I think it has a lot to do with it. However, it's worth remembering that only about 10% of sales come from data centers, so while Powell is often considered a data center energy play, that's not quite the case. Yes, this is a rapidly growing segment and could be important in the future, but its largest segment (Oil & Gas) grew 14.40% during Q1 2025, its second-largest segment (petrochemicals) grew 16.98%, and its electric utility segment grew 25.89%. The company is not dependent on data center demand.

Tariff impact

Another of the oft-mentioned issues in recent days, and one that has also played a role in the stock's decline, is Trump's tariffs on all countries. In this case, it's worth starting by mentioning that more than 80% of Powell's sales come from the United States, so the company is not as exposed to potential tariff responses that other geographies beyond Canada (another 10% of sales) may face.

Regarding the source of materials, the company stated since February that the products used are typically purchased within the country where they are sold. This "under one roof" philosophy reduces the risk of tariffs to almost zero, and between that and the majority of production and sales within the United States, the company could be considered practically immune to this effect.

The majority of our product costs are sourced domestically in each one of our geographies that we operate in. And in the event that we do encounter tariff cost headwinds, these will be passed along in the form of price through our pricing models.

Michael Metcalf - CFO.

This means the company shouldn't experience a major impact on either sales or margins for the remainder of the year, despite the two aforementioned phenomena that have developed in recent months. While Q1 2025 margins were slightly lower than in previous quarters, this is because the first quarter is typically the weakest, I'm still interested in continuing to monitor margins in the next quarter, which should be reported in late April or early May.

Valuation and risks

The reason I've decided to upgrade to buy is because the company has fallen 21%. Currently, just looking at the historical EV/EBITDA multiple, we see that we could clearly buy at multi-year lows, except for a period in 2020 following the COVID crisis. However, I don't think there's any reason this time that puts the company's sales at risk or that will deeply damage its margins.

There is one point worth mentioning: the price of crude oil has dropped considerably in recent weeks, dropping from $80 to $60. Considering that Oil & Gas is Powell's largest segment, this could reduce the attractiveness for companies in the sector to invest in Powell's electrical systems for new exploration sites.

However, if we zoom out, we can notice that the price is not in an abnormal range either; in fact, it is quite in line with the average price of the last 10 years and the price of $80 was the one above the average. I doubt that a reversion to the mean would cause projects to fall massively; however, I'm not an expert in this industry, so I cannot give a firm opinion and I would recommend you take this information into account.

Assuming sales end slightly lower than Q1 2025 (24.43% year-over-year growth during that quarter) with similar margins, the company would earn around $170 million in net income this year. With a P/E of 15, which would be below the industry average and Powell's usual range, divided by the 12.2 million shares, the shares price would already justify being worth at least 20% more than their current price and the 5-year annual return would be 14%.

Why my upgrade to buy? Because in my previous article, that same return potential was achieved with a P/E ratio of 20, which is the sector average. In this case, if it trades below the sector average, we have a 20% upside, but if reaches its average, the upside would be 60% by FY2025. That's the beauty of buying with a margin of safety, you're not relying on the ideal scenario as being true to make money.

The bottom line

Powell Industries has been dragged down by the negativity in the data center sector and the broader stock market. However, I don't see any imminent downside risk at the moment and the valuation is now much more attractive with higher potential returns without the need for an ideally high P/E multiple. Hence, my upgrade to buy recommendation.

data.title: Conduent At A Crossroads: Will Investors Recognize Earnings Growth? (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis

For most of the past decade, the share price of business processing company Conduent Incorporated (NASDAQ:CNDT) has been slipping and sliding lower and lower. It peaked in the low $20s in 2018; at the close on April 14, 2025, it closed at $2.12, roughly a tenth of what it was at its highest.

While revenue was declining along with the share price, net income and basic EPS have made significant gains. That’s left a sizeable gap between price and earnings.

Expect that earnings growth to continue this year, by something around 5%. There are multiple positive signs, including lower expenses. But whether the share price will follow remains an open question.

I now rate Conduent a Hold because I’m not sure if the markets will begin seeing the firm as an attractive investment, with its bottom-line improvements.

About Conduent

The firm provides digital business solutions and services to the commercial, government, and transportation sectors. As management described itself in the 10-K for 2024, “Each day, our solutions and services interact in the lives of millions of people in many ways - from safer, more seamless commutes that reduce congestion to streamlined benefits enrollment, digital payments, customer experiences and government healthcare claims.”

It operates globally and at the end of 2024, had about 56,000 associates, almost 40% of them in North America. Others are based in the Asia Pacific, Latin America, the Caribbean, and Europe.

As this slide from its February 2025 Investor Deck shows, the company is well established with clients:

When I wrote about the company in July 2024, I used this headline, “Conduent Selling Subsidiaries To Enable Profitable Growth”. And I noted the company was undergoing a transition with the goal of improving its growth and profitability.

In the fourth quarter and full-year 2024 results, management reported all three deals were completed, bringing in $780 million after taxes. The proceeds helped it cut its debt by half when compared with year-end 2023.

Conduent also completed a $75 million share repurchase initiative, buying back 52 million common shares, which will be a boost to earnings per share. The buy-backs included roughly 38 million from Carl Icahn and associates.

At the close on April 14, shares traded at $2.12, and it had a market cap of $330.13 million.

Competition and competitive advantages

In the 10-K, it reported heavy competition in all areas in which it does business, adding that it is a leader in many categories. Its competitiveness is based on technology, performance, quality, reliability, reputation, price, and customer service.

Named competitors include multinationals such as Accenture plc (ACN), business process outsourcing firms such as ExlService Holdings, Inc. (EXLS), federal government services providers such as Leidos Holdings, Inc. (LDOS), and transportation multinationals such as Thales S.A. (OTCPK:THLEF).

To assess its competitive advantages, we will compare its net income margin and return on equity performance with the competitors named above. First, net margins over the past decade:

Conduent leads the pack on net margin, but it has been far less consistent than the others. At the end of Q3-2023, its margin was minus 16.91%, and it has come back to put itself into contention again. It’s a similar story when we turn to return on equity:

Based on the data in these charts, I believe Conduent has a medium if not wide moat.

Latest earnings

As the two charts above indicate, its net margin and return on equity levelled out in Q4-2024 after five consecutive quarters of outstanding growth. Let’s put them in context with revenue, net income, and basic EPS over the past decade:

Again, we see a levelling after five quarters of rapid growth. But, what I find interesting here is that net income and basic EPS have trended upward, while revenue trends in the opposite direction.

Normally, I would be pleased to see net income or EPS, or both, growing more quickly than revenue. That suggests the company is becoming more efficient and/or effective in its operations. However, we’re looking at a sustained slump in revenue, one that has lasted 10 years and fallen by roughly half, from $6.66 billion in 2015 to $3.36 billion in 2024.

On the Q4 and year-end earnings call, CEO Cliff Skelton explained,

“Look, as you know, our year was characterized by some intense focus on culling the herd, as they say, for several reasons. We believe this portfolio was and perhaps continues to be too wide, resulting in cost in the center that's too high in a scenario where certain underperforming assets can drag the whole down. That's notwithstanding the fact that many growth companies typically have more scale and depth, and we're progressing towards both. The good news is that we were able to divest several assets at good multiples, allowing us to pay down the bulk of our debt and buy out the Icahn Group, thus streamlining both our strategy and our game plan.”

He added that the sees more opportunities to rationalize the portfolio, “We have assets that without significant investment are a drag to EBITDA, which we will disposition. And we have assets that have scarcity value on the outside but not have the characteristics we think are crucial to our growth and our cash flow imperatives.”

Revenue has been slipping because Conduent has been slimming its portfolio, getting rid of businesses that were not making a bottom-line contribution. I presume that this wasn’t just a problem in 2024, but a decade-long problem.

Those 2024 divestitures allowed it to cut its debt by roughly half, and thus reduce interest expenses, which leads to higher net income and earnings. This transition could be transformational.

With that context, let’s review specifics from the Q4 and full-year earnings report:

Looking at the full-year results, we see most metrics slipped again, including revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted free cash flow. But we see improvement in net income and EPS, mainly because of the divestitures and a goodwill impairment in 2023.

2024 also saw several initiatives that should help grow revenue and earnings in the future. In addition, it also won a number of awards last year, including the Newsweek Top 100 Most Loved Workplaces. Awards such as these tell us employees are motivated and productive.

It does not pay a dividend.

Conduent’s growth prospects

The earnings report also provided a full-year 2025 outlook. It is forecasting adjusted revenue of $3.200 billion - $3.30 billion, compared to FY 2024’s actual result of $3.176 billion. If Conduent hits this target, it would be the first revenue growth in a decade, a significant milestone.

It projects adjusted EBITDA of about 8.0% by year’s end, as compared to 3.9% in 2024. That would involve a doubling of the amount and percentage performance, again an important milestone if it’s achieved.

Adjusted free cash flow is expected to reach $60 to $80 million by the end of this year, compared with minus $59 million in 2024. Another big reversal, making more funds available for shareholder rewards, as well as organic and acquired growth.

Interest expense is forecast to be about $38 million by the end of the year, compared with $75 million in 2024. That, of course, reflects the reduction of debt from divestitures and will be an important contributor to free cash flow.

Restructuring costs also will be down, by about two-thirds, from $46 million in 2024 to about $15 million this year. If this target is reached, that’s another $31 million available for constructive initiatives.

And CapEx will grow, from $73 million in 2024 to about $80 million in 2025. Higher CapEx usually leads to higher revenue and earnings in the future, another positive signal.

All of this suggests to me that Conduent is about to begin reaping what it has shown in its transitional strategy. The odds are much higher now than they were a year ago that the firm will become a growth stock again, and a profitable one at that.

In this growth context, I’m not sure what to make of the Wall Street analysts’ estimates of normalized EPS:

According to the analysts who made the 2025 and 2026 estimates, normalized earnings will increase substantially in 2026, then slip back again in 2027.

The same analysts have two Down revisions and no Up revisions. Presumably, that reflects the outlook for just 2025 rather than 2025 and 2026. The revisions also are at odds with their two Strong Buy ratings.

A couple of other issues are worth noting. First, CEO Skelton said on the earnings call that changes occurring in federal government operations could be good for Conduent, “we continue to see outsourcing demand as clients continue to emphasize efficiency.”

Second, institutional investors, the so-called “smart money,” are increasing their positions in Conduent. According to Nasdaq data, 104 funds increased their positions by 14.611 million shares, while 76 decreased their holdings by 10.509 million shares. 62 institutions held their previous stakes, representing 95.135 million shares. In total, these investors held 74.31% of shares outstanding.

Both issues, and especially the growth in institutional holdings, are a bullish signal for the future.

Valuation

There hasn’t been much joy in watching Conduent’s share price since September 2018, as shown in this 10-year chart:

The most recent dip, of 10.70% reflects the broader market retreat in response to the tariff announcements. Although its operations are not directly affected by the tariffs—it is a services company, not a products company—its share price couldn’t help but be pulled down as the market declined.

The decline has pushed it into undervalued territory, with a Seeking Alpha valuation grade of A+. I agree with that assessment, but with the caveat that it’s not a satisfactory value stock unless we can expect significant bottom-line growth that will drive up the share price.

I like to set one-year price targets on the basis of EPS, since price will generally follow earnings. However, in Conduent’s case, price and basic earnings divorced in 2019 and have been living separately ever since:

The story is much the same for normalized diluted EPS and the share price. Could the failure of price to follow earnings come from the fact that Conduent’s earnings have been negative for most of the past decade? Have investors simply lost interest and given their attention to other stocks in the industry?

I don’t have answers to these questions, but it appears institutional interest has picked up, given the recent transactions we’ve seen, and as noted, they represent three-quarters of shares outstanding.

Getting back to the one-year target, I will not offer one at this time. In my previous, July 4, 2024 article, I set a target of $4.14 and gave Conduent a Buy rating. At that time, the price was $3.31, and it has since declined, despite earnings rising significantly in the past year.

It is that divergence between earnings and the share price that makes me hesitant to set a price target. When I wrote the July 2024 article, the company's most recent quarterly report, March 2024, saw basic EPS jump from $0.01 to $0.46. I thought that would be a catalyst for the share price, but I was wrong.

Subsequently, EPS more than doubled to $1.09 in Q2-2024. And again, the share price remained relatively flat. When the price does not respond to changes in earnings, I have no idea what it will do in another year.

EPS has fallen since Q2-2024, but I believe Conduent has the potential to become a profitable growth stock in the future, so I am reluctant to rate it a Sell. And so I give it a Hold.

There have been no other Seeking Alpha analyst ratings in the past 90 days. The Quant system rates it a Hold, while the Wall Street analysts have a Strong Buy. The Wall Street analysts also have an exceptionally high one-year target: $7.03:

That target seems unrealistic, for at least three reasons. First, it assumes many investors, including some institutional funds, will radically change their pessimistic opinions.

Second, it implies some massive improvement in bottom-line results over the coming year. The company itself forecasts adjusted EBITDA growth of 4.5% to 5.5%, and while adjusted EBITDA and EPS are not the same, that’s probably a reasonable EPS estimate. I think some gains should be expected, but nothing dramatic.

Third, as we’ve seen, Conduent can be pulled down by external issues such as tariffs. As I write this, I don’t see any immediate resolution of that issue, but I do see potential for trade wars and a recession.

On the flip side, it’s much easier to triple the price of a $2-something stock than a $50 or $100 stock.

Risk factors

As noted, there is uncertainty about economic conditions. Conduent may not be directly affected, still, its price could be pulled down by market-wide downdrafts, and its customers may spend less because of the existing uncertainty.

Forecasts of future results, including mine, other analysts, and the company, assume a future that is somewhat similar to the past year or two. At this point, that assumption may no longer be reasonable.

A significant portion of its revenues come from U.S. governments and their agencies. Conduent management believes this may lead to more outsourcing for greater productivity, however, departments and agencies may not have funding for that type of service.

Another important portion of its revenue comes from outsourcing. That stream of income may be disrupted if the governments and companies that use its services decide to handle those business process services internally.

The firm operates in a highly competitive industry. New technologies or business strategies could give competitors an edge that reduces its market share or profitability.

Conclusion

I see many signs that indicate Conduent Incorporated will begin experiencing consistent, profitable growth, rather than losses. The bigger question in my mind is whether the market will begin bidding up its price; it's possible institutional investors may do so, and perhaps pull along individual investors.

While I see the encouraging signs, I’m sticking with a Hold rating for now, waiting to see if quarterly profits keep increasing and how the markets react to that potential growth.

data.title: World Markets Watchlist: April 11, 2025
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Our global markets watchlist tracks nine prominent indexes from economies around the world. The list includes the S&P 500 from the United States, TSX from Canada, the FTSE 100 from England, the DAXK from Germany, the CAC 40 from France, the Nikkei 225 from Japan, the Shanghai from China, the Hang Seng from Hong Kong, and the BSE SENSEX from India. For a look at how some emerging markets across the globe stack up against each other, read our emerging markets update.

Three of the nine indexes on our world watch list have posted gains through April 14, 2025. Hong Kong’s Hang Seng is in the top spot with a year to date gain of 9.14%. Germany’s DAXK is in second with a year to date gain of 4.10% while China's Shanghai is in third with a year to date gain of 0.01%. On the opposite end, Tokyo's Nikkei 225 has posted the largest decline for the year thus far, with a 14.82% loss.

To provide additional context on where these indexes stand relative to their historical peaks, the table below shows each index’s current value, all-time peak, the date of that peak, and how far it is from that record level.

World Indexes and Recent Recessions

Let's start with a very recent chart with the latest recession. We've used February 3, 2020 for our start date (this is the official NBER recession start).

The chart below illustrates the comparative performance of world markets since March 9, 2009. The start date is arbitrary: The S&P 500, TSX, CAC 40 and BSE SENSEX hit their lows on March 9th, the Nikkei 225 on March 10th, the DAXK on March 6th, the FTSE on March 3rd, the Shanghai Composite on November 4, 2008, and the Hang Seng even earlier on October 27, 2008.

However, by aligning on the same day and using a log-scale vertical axis, we get an excellent visualization of the relative performance. I've indexed each of the eight to 800 on the March 9th start date. The callout in the upper left corner shows the percent change from the start date to the latest weekly close.

Here is the same visualization, this time starting on October 9, 2007, a previous closing high for the S&P 500. This date is also approximately the mid-point of the range of market peaks, which started on June 1st for the CAC 40 and ended on January 8, 2008 for the SENSEX.

For a longer look at the relative performance, our final chart starts at the turn of the century, again indexing each at 800 for the start date.

Examples of single country ETFs:

  • WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ)
  • WisdomTree Europe Hedged Equity Fund (HEDJ)
  • KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (KWEB)
  • iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA)
  • iShares MSCI Hong Kong ETF (EWH)
  • iShares MSCI Canada ETF (EWC)
  • SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY)

Note: I track Germany's DAXK a price-only index, instead of the more familiar DAX index (which includes dividends), for consistency with the other indexes, which do not include dividends.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ARGT: Lifting Of Currency Controls Paves The Way For The Argentine Economic Miracle
data.description:
Finally, after several months of negotiations, Argentine Economy Minister Luis Caputo announced this Friday a long-awaited agreement between Argentina and the International Monetary Fund (IMF) for an amount of $20 billion, of which approximately $12 billion will arrive as early as next week for the Argentine government to use freely. This far exceeded the consensus of analysts, who estimated that the initial IMF disbursement could be between $8 billion and $10 billion.

With the IMF agreement under its belt and the possibility of obtaining other important monetary support, the Milei government now intends to cancel the outstanding debt of the Central Bank (BCRA) that continued to generate the issuance of pesos through interest payments, as well as the lifting, starting this Monday, of the exchange controls that were still in force in the country since 2019 (cepo).

After a stellar year, Argentine stocks are under strong pressure in the first months of 2025 amid a tariff war between the United States and China that has plunged global financial markets into uncertainty, severely impacting valuations. So far this year, the Argentine equity and ADR ETF, Global X MSCI Argentina ETF (NYSEARCA:ARGT), has seen a significant correction in search of new growth catalysts and as a result of profit-taking by investors.

Despite this, the fundamentals of the Argentine economy remain solid, now marking a very important step in the implementation of the pro-market agenda of the government led by President Javier Milei. But let me first explain what's going on in Argentina and why this will be relevant in the coming years.

Macro Context

Under the exchange control regime officially implemented in 2011, during the administration of Cristina Fernández de Kirchner, with a brief interruption between 2015 and 2019 during Mauricio Macri's term, the exchange controls acted as a restriction on the free availability of dollars in the economy by the population, limiting the amount of U.S. dollars a person could purchase on the regulated official market to $200 per month.

This made both savings and transactions in this currency extremely difficult, fostering the proliferation of a black market for the sale of dollars and creating an exchange rate gap that demonstrated the price distortions prevailing in the economy. In turn, companies wishing to import from abroad or distribute profits and dividends in dollars to their foreign shareholders had to request prior authorization from the State to sell them dollars. Under this restriction, foreign companies wishing to invest in the country could not compensate their shareholders abroad with their returns, making Argentina a much less attractive investment destination.

Following the change in the exchange rate regime, the dollar will now move to a free-floating system between moving bands, abandoning the current 1% crawling peg that set the pace of depreciation of the peso against the U.S. dollar. From now on, the market itself will determine the equilibrium exchange rate through the mechanism of supply and demand for dollars by economic agents, while the dollar exchange rate will be unified, providing greater transparency to the market.

In my last article on Banco Galicia, I mentioned the possibility of this agreement being signed in April and addressed the opportunities that the lifting of the exchange rate restrictions would open up for Argentina with the influx of foreign capital into projects like Vaca Muerta and the country's real estate sector.

This new milestone in President Javier Milei's administration confirms the signal the markets had been hoping for and something many companies were expecting to send a new wave of investments to the country.

In words of Minister Caputo himself:

Ending the exchange rate controls within this macroeconomic context of fiscal and monetary policy will inevitably lead to the influx of investments that are currently pending until Argentina begins this new phase by ending these exchange rate restrictions. These investments will generate greater growth, which in turn will generate a larger surplus that will favor further tax cuts, more jobs, and higher wages.

After reorganizing public accounts based on a sharp 30% cut in public spending since the start of its administration, the Argentine government finally managed to eliminate a chronic fiscal deficit that the country had been carrying for decades, which was key to regaining the trust of the IMF technical staff. This was also achieved with economic growth, a recovery in wages, and a sharp drop in consumer prices for goods and services, which by the end of 2023 were heading towards hyperinflation.

It's also worth mentioning that one of the main drawbacks of the new measures announced by the Argentine government is some limitations on the withdrawal of dividends from previous years by foreign investors. Although companies will be able to freely buy and sell dollars from next week, they will be able to transfer dividends to their shareholders abroad only for those earnings accrued from 2025 onward. Meanwhile, for earnings prior to this year, companies will be able to choose to use their pesos to purchase a special bond issued by the Central Bank (Bopreal) that pays dollars at maturity. After that, these funds will be freely available to be sent abroad as dividends.

Mattress of Dollars

However, Argentina's macroeconomic stabilization program is not limited to the IMF and will have additional international support. In addition to the $12 billion of immediate free availability under the agreement with the IMF, Argentina is in advanced negotiations to receive another $6.1 billion from other international organizations and banks, in addition to a new repo line of credit for up to $2 billion and the renewal of the swap contract with China for up to $5 billion.

Thus, the Argentine president stated that by May, the BCRA's gross reserves are expected to be around $50 billion, of which approximately $28 billion will be available this year. This does not yet include the influx of dollars the country will receive as a result of the harvest season and the arrival of new investments, of which approximately $12 billion is already in projects covered by the new tax incentive regime (RIGI), mainly in the energy and mining sectors.

It's worth adding that this is happening amid a fiscal surplus and having effectively eliminated the unbacked issuance of pesos that was generating inflation. The Argentine central bank now has a strengthened financial and liquidity position to prevent a depreciation of the peso, conditions that didn't exist under previous programs. In fact, the BCRA itself could currently withdraw the country's entire monetary base in pesos (~AR$48T) in exchange for dollars, completely dollarizing the economy with freely available reserves.

The IMF staff projects annualized GDP growth of 5.5% for 2025 (raising its previous forecast of 5%) after a decade of economic stagnation during which the country experienced anemic levels of investment, causing factor productivity to collapse. The organization now forecasts very positive growth for the Argentine economy, driven by solid domestic demand and a carryover effect from stronger growth recorded during the second half of 2024. It also notes that growth in the medium term is expected to converge to around 3% as the stability and pro-market reforms implemented today bear fruit.

Exposure to Argentine Equity

With no growth catalysts in sight to revitalize the upward trend and after being the best ETF for the country globally in 2024, the ARGT has fallen 4% so far this year, the result of profit-taking after a meteoric rise last year and dragged down by the uncertainty prevailing in the markets during the first months of this year. However, despite the recent extreme volatility, the country's macro fundamentals remain very solid, to which is now added a potential catalyst for Argentine stocks.

ARGT has a fairly high exposure to MercadoLibre (NASDAQ:MELI), strongly correlated with the U.S. consumer discretionary sector, which has recently been severely punished by investors amid U.S.-international trade tensions. Furthermore, excluding MELI, we see significant participation in energy and financial companies, which I consider more attractive in this context. Particularly, those with exposure to the Vaca Muerta deposits and projects under development in that region of the country have the best medium- and long-term prospects. For their part, I believe Argentine banks will be among those benefiting from the arrival of investment to the country, much of which will be channeled through the local financial system.

That said, I consider ARGT a good option for gaining general exposure to the Argentine market without being overly selective, also boasting acceptable liquidity and nearly $1 billion in assets under management. On Friday, following the news of the government's lifting of foreign exchange controls, ARGT rose 5.91% after hours, closing an extremely volatile week with a strong 14% rally. However, the ETF's shares are still significantly lagging its regional peers so far this year.

Risks

The possibility that the new monetary regime could trigger a strong depreciation of the peso against the dollar early in the process represents one of the main risks, affecting the inflation levels recorded in recent months. March inflation rose 3.7% month-on-month, above the reading from previous months and an expected 2.6%, driven primarily by an increase in education and food prices. Confirmation of the upward reversal in the price index could once again generate distrust among agents regarding the feasibility of the government's monetary policy objectives and slow macroeconomic progress just months before the country's legislative elections.

Regarding the international scenario, Trump's measures could also cloud the outlook, as the intensification of trade tensions could affect global demand for commodities, negatively impacting Argentina's growth and its ability to access international capital markets. However, it should be noted that Argentine exports to the U.S. were only $6.5 billion (about 1% of GDP) in 2024, so the direct impact of the 10% tariff imposed by Washington on almost all countries in the world, including Argentina, would be less than 0.1% of GDP.

Conclusion

Despite a recent spike in inflation, the macroeconomic context remains highly favorable to Argentina. The agreement reached with the IMF, enabling the arrival of funds to strengthen the Central Bank's reserves, unlocks a wealth of investments that were pending a resolution. At the same time, the Milei administration demonstrates an unwavering commitment to carrying out its structural reform plan to restore the country's growth and restore the economic well-being it once enjoyed.

However, this does not come without internal and external challenges, which must be taken into account when investing in the Argentine market as a whole. In particular, the volatility in global markets resulting from a trade and tariff war between the United States and China has put stocks in jeopardy, especially Argentine ADRs listed on Wall Street, which have experienced sharp declines in recent weeks.

While I consider the country's fundamentals to be extraordinary, this latter aspect leads me to give ARGT a Hold rating while we wait for trade tensions to dissipate and for greater clarity regarding the trade policies of the warring powers.

data.title: Prologis: Business May Benefit From Trade War
data.description:
Financial markets are in a bad mood right now and are concerned about the trade war. But there are so many different companies trading in the U.S. market. And so, even in a trade war, there will be those companies that have more to gain than to lose. One such company I consider to be Prologis, Inc. (NYSE:PLD)

Company Portrait

Prologis excerpted from Wikipedia:

Prologis, Inc. is a real estate investment trust headquartered in San Francisco, California that invests in logistics facilities.[5] The company was formed through the merger of AMB Property Corporation and Prologis in June 2011, which made Prologis the largest industrial real estate company in the world.

The company has a huge amount of land and real estate owned. In the 2024 annual report, the following map of the company's total holdings was presented.

Prologis is the largest industrial lessor in the world. The company describes its activities as follows:

We predominately invest in logistics facilities. Our properties are typically used for distribution, storage, packaging, assembly, and light manufacturing of consumer products. The vast majority of our operating properties are used by our customers for retail and online fulfillment and business-to-business transactions.

In 2024, the company had revenue of $8.2 billion and profit of $3.7 billion. You can see more detailed figures in the table below.

With a price per share of $96, the company has a capitalization of $89 billion. The P/E ratio is 24 units. The company also has preferred stock (OTCQB:PLDGP) with a capitalization of $41 billion.

But the earnings numbers for Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT), which is what Prologis is, are not quite fair. That's because earnings take into account depreciation and completed transactions to buy or sell real estate. But it's not quite right to apply depreciation to real estate in assessing value, so there's a metric called FFO (Funds From Operations), which more accurately reflects a company's performance and cash flow. And the company's FFO in 2024 was $5.56 per share, while earnings were $4.01 per share. And FFO in 2024 compared to 2023 was down 0.9%, while earnings were up 21%. The company believes that in 2025, FFO will be $5.65 to $5.81 per share.

In the structure, Real Estate generates more than 90% of revenue. The weighted average lease term of customer contracts is 64 months. And long-term lease contracts guarantee stable cash flow performance of the company in the future.

Less than 10% of revenue is generated by the second line of business. Strategic Capital Segment includes revenues from asset management and property management services, transactional services for acquisition, disposition and leasing activity and promote revenue earned from the unconsolidated co-investment ventures.

The company has thousands of clients worldwide. Amazon (AMZN), The Home Depot (HD), FedEx (FDX), United Parcel Service (UPS), Geodis, NFI Industries, DHL, Walmart (WMT), lululemon athletica (LULU), and GigaCloud are in the top ten in terms of volume. Most of the business is in the U.S., as you can see from the table below, which shows the geography of revenues.

But here it should be said that the company often owns real estate in a share with other investors, and at the same time manages the assets of these investors, which, in fact, is the basis for the second business segment of the company, Strategic Capital Segment. Therefore, the total value of all real estate is much larger than the value of the company's assets.

Development Dynamics And Problems

In general, the company's business is clear: the company constructs buildings for industrial facilities and warehouses. Then, it either sells these facilities or sells part of the facility and further leases them out. It is a management company for investors who have also invested their funds in various properties of the company.

Of the recent trends, the company is actively building data centers. But the company's problem is that in the last year, with the exception of AI investment activity in the rest of the economy, the pace of building has been declining.

From the table below, you can see that the company had 55 construction starts in 2023, but in 2024, that number dropped to 26.

Although rental rates increased from 2021 and increased the company's rental income, this growth stopped in 2024. Plus, the U.S. had high inflation, and Fed Funds rates also increased from 2022. The high rates led to a cooling in the U.S. real estate market as a whole. It is also worth noting that yields on U.S. Treasury bonds and other instruments rose, which created a competitive alternative for real estate investments.

This affected the fact that the appetite for investing in real estate has decreased. And after a wave of rising demand for warehouses, after a pandemic in previous years, there is a cooling of the market here in 2024 as well. Because of this, the company appears to have seen a drop in new construction in 2024. Because of that, the company saw a drop in Strategic capital segment revenues.

These factors, I believe, have caused the company's stock to perform poorly over the past year despite the growth in total revenues and profits.

And of course, this year, the company's stock has fallen in value due to the general situation in the markets caused by the trade war.

Outlook

On April 16, the company will release its Q1 2025 earnings report. I don't expect any big changes, big positive or negative, compared to the stock's movement over the last month because the impact of the duty hike hasn't happened yet. Against the backdrop of the trade war, what happened in the first quarter is already in past economic conditions, so the first quarter report is already outdated, as well as all the past financial models and calculations. But most likely, if we look at the situation without the trade war, the report will probably show a continuation of the cooling trend in the industrial real estate market.

However, for investors, it is important to understand what conditions a company might find itself in during a trade war!!!!

Trump may have his own grandiose plans related to geopolitics, as I wrote about in one of my articles, but one way or another, the trade war has already started. A trade war can disrupt more familiar supply chains, which can lead to chaos where congestion in the movement of goods can occur. I think that many companies may continue to produce their goods, but the speed of processing them at customs will drop, and perhaps many may decide to wait to sell the goods in the hope that duties will be reduced. The congestion will lead to higher inventories and may overload transportation, warehousing, and logistics infrastructure.

This situation is to Prologis's advantage. What for other companies will be a problem, in the form of increased logistics costs, for Prologis will be an increase in the utilization of its warehouses. In the short term, that could give the company a very nice boost in revenue.

Trump and U.S. Industry

Donald Trump has repeatedly stated that he wants manufacturing to come back to the U.S., and that is the reason for raising duties. I don't know how feasible his wishes are, given that labor is expensive in the U.S. and not all manufacturing will benefit from moving their production to the U.S. Nevertheless, some portion of companies will eventually move their plants and factories to the U.S.

And Prologis builds real estate for industry. Moving manufacturing to the U.S. creates potential new customers for the company!!! If you take the production of some list of goods of children's toys, the production of which will be conditionally moved to the U.S. from China in the coming years. Such production is exactly the ideal client for Prologis. It would be expensive for a toy manufacturer to buy and build its own building because it is a capital-intensive investment. But to rent or build a building using the Prologis business model with equity participation will be just as profitable.

It is also worth understanding one more thing!!! To grow 1 kg of chicken, you need to spend 3 kg of feed. If you bring in finished chickens, you need to process 1 kg of loads, and if you raise chickens, you need to process 4 kg of loads. I think it works the same way in industry. Importing a good involves a one-time transportation of the good from point A to point B. Manufacturing a good may require multiple movements of raw materials and components at different stages of the production chain.

I think that if American industry increases its production by a notional $100 billion to replace Chinese imports, in terms of logistical burden, this could offset the overall drop in consumption by a multiple of that amount, as in the chicken example. In my opinion, the increase in production activity will lead to the fact that not only finished goods but also raw materials, components, and finished goods themselves will have to be transported and stored. This should increase the total load on warehouses and logistics. And this is again favorable for Prologis because it rents out warehouses. Therefore, what for other companies will be a cost for Prologis will be revenue.

It should be added that the potential easing of the Federal Reserve's monetary policy may have a positive impact on the availability of capital and may have a positive impact on the real estate market as a whole.

Risks

The main risk is that the overall downturn in the U.S. economy could be stronger than the effect I described above. But in this case, we would be talking about a full-blown prolonged economic crisis in the US, in which the entire market would fall very hard.

Logistics failures can only temporarily cause inventory to rise. In a prolonged crisis in the economy, turnover may eventually fall. Also, in a prolonged crisis, investment activity may be depressed, hindering growth in the industry.

However, I think an excessive downturn in the economy will still force Trump to hit the brakes, or the brakes may be hit by Congress with the Senate, or the Fed may turn on the printing press.

Summary

Based on the above, I can say that in the past reality, Prologis was not that interesting; it had a stable and reliable business with a strong balance sheet that was dependent on interest rates and activity in the US economy and was exposed to general cycles in the economy. But at the same time, the company did not have any prospects for strong growth beyond the general trends in the economy.

Now in a new environment where there are apparently going to be big changes. In the short term, the company may benefit from the mess in the logistics system and the resulting increase in inventories due to the resulting congestion in the movement of goods. In the long term, increased industrial activity in the U.S., even partly due to the imposition of duties, will increase demand for the company's building services and demand for warehousing, as the turnover of goods, raw materials, and components is likely to increase.

Company Shares

In this changed environment, the company's shares, which have fallen in price, at the level of about $90-100, are attractive for long-term investment. But, of course, it should be realized that in such a volatile market, it will be difficult for the company's shares to grow against the market. So I think it's only worth buying if you're investing for the long term, with the assumption that the company's shares may still fall after the rest of the market.

In terms of targets, given the volatility in the markets, I can't tell you where the bottom will be and when. I can only assume that in terms of numbers, what I described above will affect the company's performance in the second quarter at the earliest, and then perhaps my speculation will be confirmed, and the stock could turn upward.

As for upside targets, I think the stock could recover to the $125 a share price it was at earlier this year before the wave of declines in the markets, even if the market as a whole fails to recover. And under favorable circumstances, the stock could possibly exceed that price.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Castle Biosciences: Steady Revenue Stream, But Medicare Reimbursement Loss A Concern
data.description:
Castle Biosciences (NASDAQ:CSTL) develops diagnostic tests for patients with skin, gastrointestinal, and mental health conditions. One of their first tests was DecisionDx-Melanoma used in early-stage skin cancer to predict lymph node involvement and recurrence risk. That test still makes up a big chunk of revenue, but Castle has since expanded into other areas with products like DecisionDx-SCC for high-risk squamous cell carcinoma, TissueCypher for Barrett’s esophagus, and IDgenetix for personalized psychiatric prescribing.

Castle’s melanoma test is well adopted in community dermatology practices, but not as much in academic centers due to lack of guideline inclusion. Despite that, it’s adding new clinicians every year and pushing for broader use within practices already using the test. The company is also dealing with a potential Medicare reimbursement loss for its SCC test, which could take a bite out of revenue starting in Q2 2025. As they state in the latest earnings call:

In January 2025, Novitas, the Medicare administrative contract with jurisdiction over our laboratory in Pittsburgh, finalized the local coverage determination, or LCD, that included the language signifying noncoverage by Medicare for DecisionDx-SCC.

Going deeper into the SCC test, the company tells us two things – a) their SCC tests help patients by its "ability to predict the risk of metastasis impacting treatment pathways and the ability to predict responsiveness to adjuvant radiation therapy," and b) by achieving "substantial savings to the Medicare program of up to $972 million per year," as determined by "a cost-effectiveness article that was published in January 2024." In discussing the main avenues left to the company, management gave us two approaches – one through Novitas directly and the other through another contract partner, MolDX. The former approach may involve a reconsideration request, or wait for the results of a lawsuit filed by another company against Novitas on this issue.

On the flip side, TissueCypher is picking up steam fast and getting recognized in GI guidelines. Castle is well capitalized, sitting on nearly $300 million in cash, and while it’s burning a bit of cash now, it has plenty of runway to invest in growth or pursue smart acquisitions.

Currently, the company offers a number of tests, all of which are marketed as Laboratory Developed Tests (LDTs), meaning they're not exactly FDA approved, but many have Medicare coverage. Castle Biosciences has a portfolio of molecular diagnostic tests in dermatology, gastroenterology, and mental health. Its flagship product, DecisionDx-Melanoma, helps assess the risk of recurrence and lymph node involvement in early-stage melanoma. DecisionDx-SCC is used for high-risk cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma to predict metastasis and response to adjuvant radiation therapy. TissueCypher evaluates the risk of progression from Barrett’s esophagus to esophageal cancer and is gaining traction following inclusion in GI practice guidelines. IDgenetix, Castle’s pharmacogenomic test, supports medication selection in psychiatric care, though it has recently been deprioritized due to weak uptake. The company has also signaled plans to launch a gene expression test for atopic dermatitis in late 2025, pending successful validation.

Revenue and Analysis

Below is a consolidated table showing their Medicare rates and annual revenues:
